box_characteristics__handling_requiremen.at_what_rate_do_boxes_need_to_be_cut_ope: Target average of 450 boxes per hour during peak receiving (approximately 7.5 boxes per minute), with short bursts up to 600 boxes per hour when multiple trailers arrive simultaneously.
box_characteristics__handling_requiremen.growth_expectations_affecting_future_rat: We are planning for 15–20% annual growth in inbound volume over the next 3 years as we expand our direct‑to‑consumer and marketplace channels. Currently we receive an average of 7 inbound trailers per day (Mon–Sat), but our 3‑year plan projects 9–10 trailers per day during peak season (August–December). We also anticipate onboarding two additional major suppliers who ship mixed‑SKU cartons with higher piece counts per box, which will slightly increase the number of corrugated cases per trailer. Because of this, we would like the CASI‑IBOD system sized to comfortably handle up to 550–600 boxes per hour sustained, so that we are not immediately at capacity as volume grows. We expect seasonal spikes (Black Friday/Cyber Monday) where we may run extended shifts (up to 16 hours/day) to process as many as 7,000–8,000 boxes per day through the system.
box_characteristics__handling_requiremen.packed_other: Some vendors use internal corrugated dividers or molded pulp inserts for fragile kitchenware (glass canisters, ceramic bakeware). A smaller subset of shipments from overseas suppliers are packed with air pillows and kraft paper dunnage around individually poly‑bagged items. We rarely receive loose, unbagged product; at minimum, most items are in retail cartons or clear poly bags with suffocation warnings printed on them.
box_characteristics__handling_requiremen.boxes_will_be: CONVEYED TO CASI-IBOD FROM YOUR CONVEYOR
box_characteristics__handling_requiremen.product_type_within_boxes:
Inbound cartons primarily contain mid‑priced home goods and small housewares. Rough mix by volume is approximately:

• 35% kitchenware: stainless steel cookware sets, non‑stick pans in retail boxes, glass food storage containers, cutting boards, and small countertop appliances (toasters, coffee makers, air fryers). These are typically packed in vendor retail cartons, then master‑cartoned with air pillows or kraft paper.

• 25% soft home goods: cotton sheet sets, comforters, duvet covers, pillow protectors, and bath towels. Most soft goods are vacuum‑packed or poly‑bagged and then placed in corrugated cases. Because of compression, the boxes are usually firm but not overly heavy.

• 20% storage and organization: plastic storage bins (nested), closet organizers, shoe racks, and small shelving units. These shipments often have irregular shapes inside the cartons but are typically well‑protected with corrugated inserts.

• 10% decorative items: framed wall art, picture frames, decorative vases, and tabletop décor. These tend to be more fragile and may be double‑boxed or have extensive internal foam or corrugated partitions.

• 10% miscellaneous seasonal items: holiday décor, small patio accessories, and promotional bundles around Q4.

No food products, hazardous materials, or liquids in bulk are processed through this area. The main concern from a cutting standpoint is avoiding deep penetrations that could damage glass or ceramic items near the top of the case, especially on the decorative and kitchenware SKUs. Most products are in some form of inner packaging (retail boxes, foam, or poly bags), which gives us a small buffer between the case lid and the product itself.

post_cut_process__installation.what_happens_to_cut_boxes_after_leaving_:
Once the boxes exit the CASI‑IBOD, they will travel on a powered roller conveyor to a manual de‑cartoning station line. Our plan is to have two parallel lines, each staffed with 2–3 associates during peak. At these stations, operators will:

1. Fold back or remove the cut case flaps as needed.
2. Remove inner product (either full inner cartons or individual units, depending on how the vendor packs the shipment).
3. Place product into blue plastic totes (24" x 16" footprint) that are labeled with put‑away destinations or cross‑dock labels generated from our WMS.
4. Scan a representative unit from each SKU to confirm receipt and trigger inventory updates.

Empty or partially collapsed corrugated cartons will then be pushed onto an adjacent takeaway conveyor that feeds a vertical baler in the back corner of the receiving area. We currently bale cardboard twice per shift, and we expect the IBOD to increase the consistency of cut patterns, which should make cartons easier to flatten and bale.

For certain high‑volume SKUs (e.g., popular sheet sets), we may bypass the tote step and send full inner cartons directly to a pallet build area. In those cases, operators will simply remove the lid, verify counts, and transfer the inner cartons to a pallet while the remaining corrugate continues on to the baler conveyor.

We do not plan to re‑use the inbound master cartons; they are all treated as corrugated waste after product is removed. The only exception is a small number of vendor returns that may be re‑packed into a fresh carton at a separate QC station, which is physically downstream but not directly integrated with the IBOD line.
post_cut_process__installation.box_style_other: A small subset (under 5%) of shipments from one European vendor arrive in display‑ready cases with perforated tear‑away fronts. These are still technically RSC style but have die‑cut perforations along one long panel. We would like to discuss whether these should be routed through the IBOD or handled manually, as the perforations could affect how cleanly the top is cut.

current_operations__receiving_process.how_sort_product_after_boxes_cut_open:
After boxes are cut open and product is removed, items are sorted primarily by put‑away zone and, secondarily, by handling type (pallet vs. case vs. each). Our WMS (Softeon) generates ASN‑based receiving tasks that dictate whether an item goes directly to reserve pallet locations, to forward pick shelves, or to a cross‑dock staging lane.

The typical flow is:

1. Scanner at de‑cartoning station reads the carton ID or a representative unit barcode.
2. The WMS returns a destination type (e.g., "Reserve Pallet Rack Aisle 14", "Forward Pick Bin Zone C", or "Cross‑Dock Lane 3 for Outbound PO 45721").
3. Operator places product into color‑coded totes: blue for standard put‑away, yellow for cross‑dock, and red for QC/exception handling.
4. Totes are then sent via a takeaway conveyor to a central sort point where a team of staging associates separate by zone and load onto pallet jacks or carts for final put‑away.

For pallet‑quantity receipts, we sometimes leave product in inner cartons and build full pallets directly at the receiving area. Those pallets are stretch‑wrapped and labeled and then moved to reserve storage by reach truck operators.

We also have a small rework lane where damaged or short‑shipped cartons are diverted. These are tagged with an orange exception label, and the product is either quarantined or re‑packed as needed. This rework lane is adjacent to, but not directly on, the IBOD outfeed path.

Overall, the goal with the IBOD is to standardize the initial carton opening so that associates can focus on accurate scanning and sorting rather than spending time cutting boxes and dealing with safety concerns around knives.

controls_responsibility__additional_comm.other_comments:
We would prefer that CASI take primary responsibility for the controls logic related to carton detection, cut pattern selection, jam detection, and communication with our upstream and downstream conveyor controls. Our internal maintenance team is comfortable handling basic I/O checks and replacing wear parts, but we do not have a dedicated controls engineer on staff.

Integration details:

• Our WMS (Softeon) already interfaces with our existing conveyor PLCs via a middleware layer that uses REST APIs. For the IBOD, we envision a simpler integration where the system receives basic run/stop signals and reports fault conditions back to our main HMI. We do not need the IBOD to make routing decisions based on SKU; it just needs to reliably open any qualifying carton that reaches the infeed.

• We will provide network drops and power in the installation area and can share our existing line layout and PLC tag structure so CASI can propose the cleanest integration approach.

Safety and training:

• We would like CASI to include operator and maintenance training at go‑live, with clear lockout/tagout procedures and recommended PM intervals. Our current receiving staff includes a mix of English and Spanish speakers, so bilingual signage and documentation would be very helpful.

Longer term, we are open to taking more ownership of controls adjustments (e.g., speed changes, recipe tweaks) once the system is stable, but initially we would like CASI to lock down critical settings so we don’t inadvertently create unsafe or unreliable conditions by experimenting on our own.
